summary: I was born and raised in Culiacán, which is the capital city of the state of Sinaloa in Mexico. Growing up there shaped much of my early life before my family moved to the United States. Culiacán is located on the west coast by the Pacific Ocean, and it holds a special place in my heart as the place where my story began.

Sinaloa is the state in Mexico where I was born, specifically in its capital, Culiacán. It’s on the west coast by the Pacific Ocean, and it’s where my family and I lived before immigrating. My connection to Sinaloa remains strong, as it represents my roots and the culture that I carry with me even now in the United States.

Mexico is my home country where I was born and raised until I was nine years old. My family and I come from Culiacán, Sinaloa, and we brought with us the culture and traditions of Mexico when we moved to the United States in 1999. Even though I live here now, I still feel deeply connected to Mexico through my family, faith, and cultural practices.

My family and I arrived in St. James, Minnesota in 1999 when I was nine years old. We came to stay temporarily with an uncle, but we ended up staying longer for work opportunities, even after our visas expired. St. James became the place where we began building a new life in the United States, facing challenges but also growing as a family.

The United States is where my family and I settled after leaving Mexico in 1999. Living here has been a story of survival and adjustment for us, especially as undocumented migrants. Despite the challenges, I graduated from Gustavus Adolphus College in 2012 and became an activist for undocumented migrants, working to improve our community and rights here.

I graduated from Gustavus Adolphus College in 2012, which was a significant milestone in my life. Attending this college helped me achieve my educational goals despite the difficulties my family faced as immigrants. It was a place where I grew academically and personally, and it empowered me to become an activist for undocumented migrants in the United States.

La Virgen de Guadalupe symbolizes strong women to me and represents the strength of the women in my family. I often think of her when I reflect on my mother’s courage in making the decision to come to the United States. Carrying an image of La Virgen reminds me of the resilience and leadership of the women who have shaped my life and inspired me to keep striving.

I was interviewed at the University of Minnesota, Twin Cities Campus, which is where I shared my immigration story in 2013. Although I did not attend this university, it served as an important place for me to reflect on my experiences and advocate for immigrant rights. The University of Minnesota provided a platform for my voice to be heard in the broader community.
